serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of honey lemon drink is approximately 350 g, providing about 1,064 kcal and 288 g of sugar — a very high-sugar serving suitable only for occasional consumption.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Lemon — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (cr_id G00010466)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy (304.0 kcal), protein (0.3 g), fat (0.0 g), carbohydrate (82.4 g), sugar (82.12 g), sodium (4.0 mg), dietary fibre (0.2 g), potassium (52.0 mg), calcium (6.0 mg), magnesium (2.0 mg), iron (0.42 mg), and cholesterol (0.0 mg) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database, cr_id G00010466, confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.0 g) and trans fat (0.0 g) are consistent with the known composition of honey and lemon juice — both contain no fat — and are confirmed zero by HPB Foodo data. Vitamin C (4.29 mg/100 g) is consistent with lemon juice contribution and is supported by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lemon juice (FDC ID 167747), which reports 38.7 mg/100 g for undiluted lemon juice; the lower value in this diluted honey-lemon preparation is plausible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB Foodo for this item are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Honey, GI 58
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Honey lemon drink derives virtually all of its carbohydrate from honey (82.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g; lemon juice contributes negligible carbohydrate). GI of 58 is the published value for honey from the University of Sydney GI Database as cited in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. This is an approved peer-reviewed source. Lemon juice does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (58 × 82.4 × 3.5) / 100 = 47.8 — classified as very high GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid honey lemon drink; the 82.1 g/100 g sugar load drives hyperinsulinaemia and renal sodium retention, worsening blood pressure control — replace with unsweetened lemon water or herbal tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 82.1 g/100 g — dominated by honey fructose and glucose — with negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g) to moderate glycaemic absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honey lemon drink is contraindicated for people with Type 2 Diabetes; a single 350 g glass delivers approximately 288 g of sugar with a glycaemic load of ~47.8, causing rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spikes — unsweetened lemon water is the recommended substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high free-sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) associated with elevated triglycerides, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid honey lemon drink; the concentrated fructose-glucose load from honey promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and visceral adiposity — limit to rare, small-volume use or eliminate entirely in favour of unsweetened beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar concentration (82.1 g/100 g) promoting pro-inflammatory mediators that overwhelm any anti-inflammatory benefit from lemon vitamin C
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid honey lemon drink at this concentration; the dominant honey sugar load actively promotes systemic inflammation — unsweetened green tea with lemon is a suitable anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Lemon Drink is a popular Singaporean beverage made by dissolving honey in warm or cold water with freshly squeezed lemon juice. It is widely sold at hawker centres, cafes, and bubble tea shops across Singapore, and is also commonly prepared at home as a soothing drink. The drink has a sweet, tangy flavour profile with the floral sweetness of honey balanced by the tartness of lemon. Its texture is thin and liquid, similar to sweetened water. Nutritionally, it is dominated by honey, which contributes approximately 82 g of sugar per 100 g of the drink — primarily fructose and glucose — making it one of the highest-sugar beverages in the Singapore food database. It provides 304 kcal per 100 g and 1,064 kcal per standard 350 g glass. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.2 g/100 g, and protein is minimal at 0.3 g/100 g. Fat is naturally zero as honey and lemon juice contain no fat by composition, confirmed by HPB Foodo. Vitamin C is present at 4.29 mg/100 g from lemon juice. The drink has a glycaemic index of approximately 58 (medium, based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) but a very high glycaemic load of ~47.8 per 350 g serving due to the large carbohydrate mass. It is classified as high concern for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. It is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, but not vegan due to honey. Common serving sizes are 350 g (1 glass) and 450 g (1 large cup). The food group has been corrected from the input record's 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients' to 'Beverages / Hot and cold beverages — sweetened', which accurately reflects the nature of this drink.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id G00010466, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and trans fat confirmed zero consistent with honey and lemon juice composition. Vitamin C value consistent with lemon juice contribution and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167747). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero: honey and lemon juice contain no fat by composition, confirmed by HPB Foodo source. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo data and consistent with honey and lemon juice composition. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo data and consistent with honey composition. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal fat. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB Foodo for this item. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this item; limited published data for this specific honey-lemon preparation in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo as of 2026. Input record listed food_group as 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients' which is incorrect for a beverage; corrected to 'Beverages / Hot and cold beverages — sweetened'. Input portions contained two duplicate '1 glass / 350 g' entries; deduplicated to one default portion. Honey is not vegan; dietary flag is_vegan set to false. Nutrient source is HPB Foodo Singapore (approved primary regional source); vitamin C value cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as secondary validation. GI source is University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care (approved GI-specific source).
